questions.prompt: What's the production-grade pattern for Kubernetes secret management?

choices.text: External secret store as source of truth, External Secrets Operator (ESO) syncing to Kubernetes Secrets, Pods consuming K8s Secrets as env vars or mounted files. Real secrets live in AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GCP Secret Manager — encrypted with KMS/HSM, rotation-capable, audited per-read. ESO with IRSA (on EKS) fetches them and writes K8s Secrets per namespace. Pods consume normally. Rotation in the external store propagates via ESO. The K8s Secret is delivery infrastructure; the external store is governance. This pattern gives proper encryption, rotation, audit, and cross-environment access — none of which K8s Secrets provide natively. The legacy alternative (Secrets in git, even base64-encoded) leaks through repo history, laptop clones, and overly-permissive access. Modern production stacks don't keep secrets in git.

questions.explanation: External store + ESO + IRSA is the modern K8s secret pattern. Secrets in git is a leak waiting to happen.

questions.prompt: A team updates a ConfigMap but the Pods don't pick up the new values. What's happening and how to fix?

choices.text: ConfigMaps mounted as environment variables don't update — env vars are set at Pod startup and don't change. ConfigMaps mounted as volumes do update (with a delay), but the application has to re-read the file. Fix options: (1) mount as volume + app-level file watch/reload; (2) restart the Deployment to pick up new env vars (`kubectl rollout restart deployment/web`); (3) use versioned ConfigMap names (`config-v3`) and update the Deployment to reference the new name — this triggers rolling update automatically; (4) install Reloader, an open-source controller that watches ConfigMaps and Secrets and automatically restarts referencing Deployments on change. For most workloads, restart on config change is acceptable; for rotation-critical secrets (database passwords, API tokens with strict TTL), implement app-level hot reload from mounted files. The 'I changed the ConfigMap and nothing happened' surprise is one of the most common K8s configuration gotchas — solved once a team has chosen their reload pattern.

questions.explanation: Env-var ConfigMaps don't auto-reload; mounted volumes do (with delay). Pick a reload strategy: restart Pods, version ConfigMap names, or use Reloader.
